channel.title: NYT > Most Recent Headlines
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Para Jennifer Lopez y Brett Goldstein, el romance lo es todo
item.description: La superestrella nacida en el Bronx y el comediante británico, que protagonizan juntos la comedia de Netflix “Turbulencia en la oficina”, tienen más cosas en común de lo que crees.
item.dc:creator: Ashley Spencer

media:content.media:credit: Sela Shiloni for The New York Times

item.title: A New Governor Faces Her First Crisis Amid Tear Gas and Tough Decisions
item.description: Gov. Mikie Sherrill dispatched the New Jersey State Police outside the Delaney Hall migrant detention center after federal officials said they would flood the area with armed agents. Not everyone approved.
item.dc:creator: Tracey Tully

media:content.media:credit: Dakota Santiago for The New York Times
media:content.media:description: Gov. Mikie Sherrill of New Jersey and Senator Andy Kim tried to visit the Delaney Hall immigration detention center on Memorial Day. Senator Kim was allowed to enter. The governor wasn’t.

item.title: ‘He Took Something From His Bag and Dropped to One Knee’
item.description: A slow-motion street scene, a momentous selfie in Central Park and more reader tales of New York City in this week’s Metropolitan Diary.

item.title: They Have Yet to Sign a Lease. But They’re Furious Over $3,100 Rents.
item.description: Climate change. Gun violence. War. Generations of young people have organized over issues they fear threaten the future. Will housing be the next cause?
item.dc:creator: Troy Closson

media:content.media:credit: Roshni Khatri for The New York Times
media:content.media:description: Angelo Mazza, who lives with his family in Astoria, Queens, has watched an affordability crisis descend on New York, his hometown.

item.title: NYT Spelling Bee Answers for June 7, 2026
item.description: Feeling stuck on today’s puzzle? We can help.
item.dc:creator: New York Times Games

item.title: Lo que Shannon Elizabeth piensa de haber sido la ‘chica sexy’ de los 2000
item.description: La actriz ayudó a definir éxitos de taquilla como “American Pie” y favoritos de culto como “Jay y el silencioso Bob”. Ahora, al entrar en un nuevo capítulo público, reflexiona sobre esa época.
item.dc:creator: Maya Salam
item.category: Jay and Silent Bob Strike Back (Movie)

media:content.media:credit: Thea Traff para The New York Times

item.title: Erdogan and Putin, the End of an Unlikely Partnership
item.description: Russia’s decline has given Turkey the freedom to pursue its interests, and Ukraine is the beneficiary.
item.dc:creator: Gonul Tol

media:content.media:credit: Pool photo by Alexander Zemlianichenko

item.title: Who Will Account for Another Foreign Policy Failure?
item.description: The American foreign policy class should shed its addiction to military force.
item.dc:creator: Peter Beinart
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
American Enterprise Institute for Public Policy Research

item.title: Member of Neo-Nazi Party Reaches Mayoral Runoff in German Town
item.description: Since the fall of the Third Reich, German voters have shunned mayoral candidates from neo-Nazi parties. That could change on Sunday in a small town in the country’s east.
item.dc:creator: Christopher F. Schuetze

media:content.media:credit: Lena Mucha for The New York Times
media:content.media:description: A billboard for the mayoral candidate Stefan Hartung hangs over the main square of Aue-Bad Schlema, which is at the heart of Germany’s debate over immigration.

item.title: Can the N.F.L. Win Fans in South Korea With Flag Football?
item.description: The inclusion of flag football in the next Olympic Games has spurred optimism about the prospects of American football in South Korea, where it has languished for decades.
item.dc:creator: Max Kim and Chang W. Lee

media:content.media:credit: Chang W. Lee/The New York Times
media:content.media:description: The size of South Korea’s football fandom is a mystery, even to the N.F.L. A common estimate is 150,000, the number of subscribers to a popular local football YouTuber.

item.title: Barry Manilow Can’t Smile Without Fans’ Help on This Song
item.description: The singer has bigger hits but “Can’t Smile Without You” has become a personal touchstone for fans who have helped him perform it live.
item.dc:creator: Rob Tannenbaum

media:content.media:credit: Leon Bennett/Getty Images
media:content.media:description: Manilow, 82, is slated to resume his “farewell tour” later this month after postponing dates amid a bout with lung cancer.

item.title: Cómo retratan las series de esta temporada el trabajo sexual en internet
item.description: El trabajo sexual virtual no es nada nuevo en la televisión. Pero este año es más visible, e incluso central en series como ‘Euphoria’, ‘Margo tiene problemas de dinero’ y ‘Máximo placer garantizado’.
item.dc:creator: Alexis Soloski

item.title: Quote of the Day: The Devil Who Mortifies Deadbeats in Venezuela
item.description: Quotation of the Day for Sunday, June 7.

item.title: Corrections: June 7, 2026
item.description: Corrections that appeared in print on Sunday, June 7, 2026.

item.title: Bruce Springsteen Opens His Music Center
item.description: Jon Bon Jovi, Public Enemy and others joined him to ring in the Bruce Springsteen Center for American Music, a $50 million archive and cultural hub.
item.dc:creator: Nick Corasaniti

media:content.media:credit: Hannah Beier for The New York Times
media:content.media:description: Bruce Springsteen greets Jon Bon Jovi at a ceremony for the Bruce Springsteen Center for American Music in Long Branch, N.J., on Saturday.

item.title: Despite Protest, Ye Takes the Stage for Thousands of European Fans
item.description: Nearly 40,000 fans came out to hear the rapper formerly known as Kanye West in the Netherlands on Saturday, even as other European countries had canceled his concerts.
item.dc:creator: Claire Moses

media:content.media:credit: Mouneb Taim/Associated Press, via Ap Photo/Mouneb Taim
media:content.media:description: “This crowd is too crazy,” Ye said inside the GelreDome stadium in Arnhem, the Netherlands, on Saturday.

item.title: At Least 12 People Shot Near Street Festival in Toledo, Ohio, Police Say
item.description: Two people appeared to have fired at each other near the Old West End Festival, striking bystanders, the police said. No fatalities have been reported so far.
item.dc:creator: Mark Walker

media:content.media:credit: Paul Sancya/Associated Press
media:content.media:description: Police in Toledo, Ohio, said that multiple people were shot on Saturday afternoon near a community street festival.

item.title: Bernadette Chirac, Formidable Ex-First Lady of France, Dies at 93
item.description: Long seen as the cool, coifed wife of the president, she emerged as a political player in her own right, as well as a relentless champion of charities.
item.dc:creator: Trip Gabriel

media:content.media:credit: Christophe Ena/Associated Press
media:content.media:description: Bernadette Chirac in 2006.

item.title: Senators Warn of Intelligence Gaps if Surveillance Program Expires
item.description: Two senior Republicans urged the Trump administration to prepare for the possible expiration of a contentious intelligence-gathering authority.
item.dc:creator: Robert Jimison

media:content.media:credit: Demetrius Freeman for The New York Times
media:content.media:description: Senator Tom Cotton, Republican of Arkansas, warned the Trump administration on Saturday to prepare for the possible expiration of a key tool for U.S. government surveillance.

item.title: An Uncertain Win for Immigrants Seeking to Stay in U.S.
item.description: After a judge’s ruling, there was a sense of renewed hope that immigration applications that were put on hold would move forward. But how soon that would happen was unknown.
item.dc:creator: Jesus Jiménez

media:content.media:credit: Salwan Georges for The New York Times
media:content.media:description: Immigrants waited in a hallway for their hearings at the Annandale Immigration Court last month.

item.title: Ahead of World Cup, Two Generations of Haitian Players Unite
item.description: A player from Haiti’s 1974 World Cup team, the last to qualify for the tournament, met with the co-captain of this year’s squad in South Florida.
item.dc:creator: Patricia Mazzei

media:content.media:credit: Scott McIntyre for The New York Times
media:content.media:description: Ricardo Adé, Haitian professional soccer player for the country’s national team, right, greets former Haitian national team player Ernst Jean-Baptiste at a restaurant in Miami, Fla., on Saturday.

item.title: How Our Theater Reporter Makes His Tony Award Predictions
item.description: Michael Paulson, the theater reporter for The New York Times, has seen every production eligible for a Tony Award in the past decade. He explains the rigorous reporting process behind his predictions.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times

item.title: Kennedy Center Loses Case Against Musician Who Canceled Over Trump Naming Dispute
item.description: A judge sided with jazz performer Chuck Redd, who canceled a 2025 holiday concert after President Trump’s name was added to the building.
item.dc:creator: Mark Walker
item.category: Kennedy, John F, Center for the Performing Arts

media:content.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
media:content.media:description: A judge threw out a lawsuit filed by the John F. Kennedy Center against a jazz musician who canceled a Christmas Eve performance.

item.title: Ukraine Strikes St. Petersburg in Long-Range Drone Attack
item.description: It was the second such attack in days and came just hours after President Vladimir V. Putin spoke at an economic forum in the city.
item.dc:creator: Neil MacFarquhar

media:content.media:credit: Anastasia Barashkova/Reuters
media:content.media:description: President Vladimir V. Putin of Russia spoke Friday at a session of the St. Petersburg International Economic Forum.

item.title: Split Seconds
item.description: Adam Wagner and Simeon Seigel combine forces on a puzzle that combines forces on its solvers.
item.dc:creator: Caitlin Lovinger

media:content.media:credit: David Malosh for The New York Times. Food stylist: Simon Andrews.
media:content.media:description: This solve is bananas!

item.title: Barge Hits Maryland Rail Bridge Along Northeast Corridor
item.description: Amtrak said crews were conducting a safety assessment for the bridge, which crosses the Susquehanna River in Maryland. The U.S. Coast Guard reported no injuries.
item.dc:creator: Orlando Mayorquín

media:content.media:credit: Associated Press
media:content.media:description: An Amtrak train passing over the Susquehanna River rail bridge between Perryville and Havre de Grace, Md.

item.title: Bob Packwood, Senator Forced to Quit in Sex Scandal, Dies at 93
item.description: An unorthodox Republican, he helped shape tax policy and women’s rights legislation before resigning in 1995 amid accusations by more than 20 women of sexual misconduct.
item.dc:creator: Katharine Q. Seelye

media:content.media:credit: Dirck Halstead/Getty Images
media:content.media:description: Bob Packwood in 1985. Although reports of his behavior toward women had circulated in Oregon for years, they did not surface nationally until 1992.

item.title: Alan Riding, Times Correspondent in Latin America and Paris, Dies at 82
item.description: He was a cosmopolitan observer and interpreter of societies he knew firsthand, whether writing about war in Nicaragua or the history and cultural salons of France.
item.dc:creator: Alan Cowell

item.title: Protester Hit by Car at Newark ICE Detention Center
item.description: A vehicle hit a protester on Friday as it was leaving Delaney Hall, an immigration detention center in Newark that has been the site of protests since late May.
item.dc:creator: Cynthia Silva

media:content.media:credit: Lexi Parra/The New York Times
media:content.media:description: On Friday evening, a day after Mayor Ras Baraka of Newark decided to scale back police presence at Delaney Hall, a fight broke out between protesters and security guards.

item.title: An Edith Wharton Short Story Is Published About 100 Years Later
item.description: The short story, which is set during World War I, is believed to have been printed for the first time on Friday. The story is thought to have been written no earlier than July 1918.
item.dc:creator: Emmett Lindner
item.category: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

media:content.media:credit: Associated Press
media:content.media:description: Edith Wharton in France in 1923. Famous for her sharp satires of New York society in the Gilded Age, Wharton spent World War I working on the front lines of French relief efforts.

item.title: C.I.A. Officer Found With Gold Bars Said to Have Created Fake Spy Program
item.description: A federal judge has ruled that David Rush, the C.I.A. employee, must remain in detention. U.S. officials say Mr. Rush funneled millions in federal funds to himself.
item.dc:creator: Julian E. Barnes and Edward Wong

media:content.media:credit: Tom Brenner for The New York Times
media:content.media:description: The George Bush Center for Intelligence at the Central Intelligence Agency in Langley, Va.

item.title: 17 Arrested, 1 Officer Injured in N.Y.C. Celebrations of Knicks’ Game 2 Win
item.description: The police took dozens of people into custody this week after N.B.A. finals watch parties in Midtown Manhattan grew unruly.
item.dc:creator: Maia Coleman

media:content.media:credit: Vincent Alban for The New York Times
media:content.media:description: The New York Police Department said it took 26 people into custody at a viewing party outside Madison Square Garden.

item.title: El papa León se prepara para enfrentar la política polarizada de España
item.description: León llegó a España el sábado para una visita de una semana en la que se reunirá con inmigrantes y podría recibir críticas de Vox, el partido de extrema derecha español.
item.dc:creator: Jason Horowitz

media:content.media:credit: Pierre-Philippe Marcou/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
media:content.media:description: El papa León XIV en el Palacio Real de Madrid el sábado, al iniciar una visita a España.

item.title: Trump’s Defense Department Sees Growing Espionage Threat From Israel
item.description: The Defense Department has increased the counterintelligence threat assessment to its highest level, and Israel is believed to have eavesdropped on American negotiations with Iran.
item.dc:creator: Julian E. Barnes and Eric Schmitt
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces

media:content.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
media:content.media:description: President Trump and Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu last December. The United States and Israel were largely aligned at the war’s outset, with Mr. Trump endorsing Mr. Netanyahu’s goal to oust the Iranian government.

item.title: N.Y. Lawmakers Move to Pause Data Centers and Curb Surveillance Pricing
item.description: The state also passed legislation that begins a two-year process to allow lawmakers to draw new congressional district lines more often. Efforts to contain plastics stalled for a third year.
item.dc:creator: Grace Ashford

media:content.media:credit: Hans Pennink/Associated Press
media:content.media:description: Despite a flurry of bills passed by state lawmakers in the last week of session, there was a sense that the prolonged budget negotiations made it difficult to do more.

item.title: Madonna Premieres ‘Confessions II,’ a Star-Studded Short Film, at Tribeca Film Festival
item.description: “Confessions II” features songs from the pop star’s coming album of the same name. At one point, green lasers shoot from the pelvises of gyrating women.
item.dc:creator: Derrick Bryson Taylor

media:content.media:credit: Brendan Mcdermid/Reuters
media:content.media:description: Madonna during a surprise concert in Times Square on Thursday. “I came to this earth to be a doer, not a watcher,” she said after her new film premiered on Friday.

item.title: The Bidens Return With New Book and South Dakota Speech Targeting Trump
item.description: The re-emergence of the former first family has been fraught for some Democrats who just want to move on.
item.dc:creator: Shawn McCreesh

media:content.media:credit: Matt Kelley/Associated Press
media:content.media:description: Former President Joseph R. Biden Jr. at a South Carolina Democratic Party rally in February.

item.title: Escape From Trumpism
item.description: The best pathway out is to make Congress great again.
item.dc:creator: Jamelle Bouie

media:content.media:credit: Christopher Lee for The New York Times

item.title: Infant Killed as Israeli Military Fires on Car in West Bank, Palestinian Officials Say
item.description: Israel’s military said a soldier opened fire after Israeli troops “perceived a vehicle accelerating toward them.” The baby’s grandmother, who was in the car, disputed that account.
item.dc:creator: Aaron Boxerman

media:content.media:credit: Mahmoud Illean/Associated Press
media:content.media:description: Fahd Abu Haykal during the funeral of his 7-month-old son, Sam, in Hebron in the Israeli-occupied West Bank on Saturday.

item.title: It’s Gazpacho Time
item.description: Julia Moskin’s best gazpacho lives up to its name, with five stars and over 16,000 reviews.
item.dc:creator: Mia Leimkuhler

media:content.media:credit: Christopher Simpson for The New York Times. Food Stylist: Simon Andrews. Prop Stylist: Paige Hicks.
media:content.media:description: Julia Moskin’s best gazpacho.

item.title: Celebrities Love Bike Dates. Should You Try One, Too?
item.description: Celebrities have recently been hitting the bike lane with their beaus, but the “Bicycle Boy” was once branded a type to be avoided.
item.dc:creator: Gina Cherelus
item.category: Love Story: John F Kennedy Jr & Carolyn Bessette (TV Program)

media:content.media:credit: Lawrence Schwartzwald/Sygma, via Getty Images
media:content.media:description: A 1995 “Sex and the City” column warning of the pitfalls of dating a “Bicycle Boy” granted a technical exception to John F. Kennedy Jr., “New York’s most famous and sought-after bike-riding bachelor.”

item.title: I’ve Been Depriving You of Cookies
item.description: In repentance, I offer you frosted sugar cookies, double chocolate chip cookies and peanut butter-miso cookies.
item.dc:creator: Vaughn Vreeland

media:content.media:credit: Yossy Arefi for The New York Times (Photography and Styling)
media:content.media:description: Eric Kim’s frosted sugar cookies.

item.title: Israeli Strike Kills 3 Lebanese Soldiers, Days After Truce Was Signed
item.description: Israel invaded Lebanon and occupied parts of the country to fight Hezbollah, an Iran-backed armed group, but its military offensive has drawn in others.
item.dc:creator: Euan Ward
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

media:content.media:credit: Reuters
media:content.media:description: Smoke after a strike near Nabatieh, in southern Lebanon, on Saturday.

item.title: A Viral Gen-Z Protest Movement Draws Thousands to India’s Capital
item.description: The Cockroach Janta Party started as a joke, but it quickly became a way for many Indians to express their frustration with the system.
item.dc:creator: Anupreeta Das and Suhasini Raj

item.title: Sorry, I’m Not Available. Talk to the A.I. Version of Me.
item.description: The hot new productivity hack for C.E.O.s and Harvard professors? A.I. twins that answer questions and attend meetings.
item.dc:creator: Sarah Kessler

media:content.media:credit: Mathieu Labrecque

item.title: 5 Unsung Heroes Who Carried the Memory of D-Day
item.description: A journalist, a medic, a weather forecaster, a Medal of Honor recipient, a French commando: Their efforts showed that courage transcends race, class and gender.
item.dc:creator: Amisha Padnani and Ash Wu

item.title: Graham Platner and the Rise of the ‘Dirtbag’ Democrat
item.description: And what the Maine candidate reveals about politics today.
item.dc:creator: Jamelle Bouie, Michelle Cottle, David French and Derek Arthur

media:content.media:credit: Illustration by The New York Times; photo by Joe Raedle/Getty

item.title: The States Where Life Is Better
item.description: Trust, well-being and mental health are all down in America. But some states are better to live in than others, according to a new study.
item.dc:creator: Nicholas Kristof

media:content.media:credit: Eric Ruby

item.title: Norma Jeane’s Still Got It!
item.description: Some continue to like it hot.
item.dc:creator: Maureen Dowd

media:content.media:credit: Philippe Halsman/Magnum Photos

item.title: When Is It Wrong to Use A.I.?
item.description: How to criticize an inevitable disruption.
item.dc:creator: Ross Douthat

media:content.media:credit: Camillo Pasquarelli for The New York Times
